quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Newton's Law of Universal Gravitation"?

options.text: F = Gm₁m₂/r²; every pair of masses attracts each other with a force proportional to the product of masses and inversely proportional to the square of distance
τ = -mgd sinθ for a pendulum; for small θ, τ ≈ -mgdθ, giving angular SHM with ω = √(mgd/I)
A system of two masses connected by a string over a pulley; a = (m₁ - m₂)g/(m₁ + m₂) for an ideal massless pulley
ω = ω₀ + αt, θ = θ₀ + ω₀t + ½αt², ω² = ω₀² + 2α(θ - θ₀) for constant angular acceleration

quiz_questions.explanation: "Newton's Law of Universal Gravitation" is defined as: F = Gm₁m₂/r²; every pair of masses attracts each other with a force proportional to the product of masses and inversely proportional to the square of distance. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Gravitational constant"?

options.text: τ = r × F; magnitude τ = rF sinθ; the rotational effectiveness of a force, measured in N·m
A dimensionless vector of magnitude 1 indicating direction; î, ĵ, k̂ denote the x, y, z directions respectively
G = 6.674 × 10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg²; a universal fundamental constant measured by Cavendish in 1798
A×B; a vector perpendicular to both A and B with magnitude ABsinθ; used in calculating torque and magnetic force

quiz_questions.explanation: "Gravitational constant" is defined as: G = 6.674 × 10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg²; a universal fundamental constant measured by Cavendish in 1798. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Gravitational field"?

options.text: g = GM/r²; the gravitational acceleration at distance r from the center of a mass M
U = -GMm/r; defined as zero at infinite separation; always negative for bound systems
A force whose work is path-independent; equivalently, the work around any closed path is zero; examples: gravity, spring force
L = Iω; the rotational analog of linear momentum, measured in kg·m²/s

quiz_questions.explanation: "Gravitational field" is defined as: g = GM/r²; the gravitational acceleration at distance r from the center of a mass M. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Superposition principle"?

options.text: The gravitational force on an object is the vector sum of the gravitational forces from all other masses
J = ∫F dt = Δp; the integral of force over time equals the change in momentum; measured in N·s
F_net = ma or F_net = dp/dt; the net force equals mass times acceleration, or equivalently the rate of change of momentum
v_min = √(gr); the minimum speed at the top of a vertical loop to maintain contact (when N = 0)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Superposition principle" is defined as: The gravitational force on an object is the vector sum of the gravitational forces from all other masses. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Shell theorem"?

options.text: A system on which no net external force acts; internal forces cancel by Newton's Third Law
v(t) = dx/dt; the derivative of position with respect to time, giving the rate and direction of motion at each instant
I = Σm_i r_i²; sum of each particle's mass times the square of its distance from the rotation axis
A uniform spherical shell attracts external masses as if all its mass were at the center; inside the shell, gravitational force from the shell is zero

quiz_questions.explanation: "Shell theorem" is defined as: A uniform spherical shell attracts external masses as if all its mass were at the center; inside the shell, gravitational force from the shell is zero. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "F = Gm₁m₂/r²; every pair of masses attracts each other with a force proportional to the product of masses and inversely proportional to the square of distance"?

options.text: Gravitational PE (near Earth)
Newton's Law of Universal Gravitation
Velocity and acceleration in SHM
Conservation of mechanical energy

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Newton's Law of Universal Gravitation".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "G = 6.674 × 10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg²; a universal fundamental constant measured by Cavendish in 1798"?

options.text: Center of mass reference frame
Gravitational constant
Average power
Net force

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Gravitational constant".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "g = GM/r²; the gravitational acceleration at distance r from the center of a mass M"?

options.text: Gravitational field
Common moments of inertia
Work by a spring
Conservation of mechanical energy

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Gravitational field".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The gravitational force on an object is the vector sum of the gravitational forces from all other masses"?

options.text: Non-conservative work
Free-body diagram
Superposition principle
Total orbital energy

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Superposition principle".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A uniform spherical shell attracts external masses as if all its mass were at the center; inside the shell, gravitational force from the shell is zero"?

options.text: Elastic PE
Instantaneous acceleration
Shell theorem
Work-Energy Theorem

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Shell theorem".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Universal Gravitation is accurate?

options.text: Universal Gravitation states that every mass attracts every other mass with force F = Gm₁m₂/r², where G = 6.674 × 10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg².
Conservative Forces and Potential Energy connect force to potential energy through F = -dU/dr (in one dimension) or F = -∇U (in three dimensions).
Newton's Second Law in its most general form states F_net = dp/dt, which reduces to F = ma when mass is constant.
The critical condition at the top of a vertical loop (minimum speed for maintaining contact) occurs when the normal force equals zero: mg = mv²/r, giving v_min = √(gr).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Universal Gravitation as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Universal Gravitation?

options.text: Free-body diagrams identify all forces acting on an object, enabling systematic application of F = ma in each coordinate direction.
Wave properties—wavelength λ, frequency f, speed v = λf, and amplitude—are described mathematically by y(x,t) = A sin(kx - ωt), linking oscillation parameters to wave propagation.
Momentum is the product of mass and velocity (p = mv) and represents the quantity of motion of an object.
This inverse-square law governs planetary orbits, satellite motion, and tidal effects.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Universal Gravitation as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Universal Gravitation?

options.text: Rotational Motion describes objects spinning about an axis using angular analogs of linear quantities: angular position θ, angular velocity ω = dθ/dt, and angular acceleration α = dω/dt.
Orbital Motion applies Newton's Second Law with gravitational force to derive orbital parameters.
The gravitational field g = GM/r² at distance r from a mass M gives the local acceleration due to gravity, explaining why g varies with altitude and location on Earth.
When non-conservative forces (friction, air resistance) are present, the generalized equation becomes KE₁ + PE₁ + W_nc = KE₂ + PE₂, where W_nc represents energy converted to or from thermal energy.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Universal Gravitation as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Universal Gravitation?

options.text: Orbital Motion applies Newton's Second Law with gravitational force to derive orbital parameters.
For constant angular acceleration, the rotational kinematic equations parallel their linear counterparts.
Universal Gravitation states that every mass attracts every other mass with force F = Gm₁m₂/r², where G = 6.674 × 10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg².
Work and Kinetic Energy defines work rigorously using calculus: W = ∫F·dr, the line integral of force along the displacement path.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Universal Gravitation as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Universal Gravitation?

options.text: This inverse-square law governs planetary orbits, satellite motion, and tidal effects.
Work and Kinetic Energy defines work rigorously using calculus: W = ∫F·dr, the line integral of force along the displacement path.
The relationship between linear and angular quantities (v = rω, a_t = rα, a_c = rω²) connects translational and rotational motion.
AP Physics C emphasizes setting up and evaluating these integrals.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Universal Gravitation as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Universal Gravitation?

options.text: Designing roller coasters using calculus-based kinematics to ensure smooth transitions between acceleration phases and safe g-force limits
Structural engineering resolving forces on bridge cables into horizontal and vertical components to analyze tension and compression in each member
GPS satellite corrections accounting for the variation of g with altitude to maintain precise timing and positioning
Spacecraft docking maneuvers calculating the center of mass of the combined vehicle to plan thruster firings for orbit adjustments

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Universal Gravitation.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Universal Gravitation?

options.text: Stellar evolution: collapsing gas clouds spin faster as they shrink (I decreases → ω increases), explaining the rapid rotation of neutron stars
Spin cycle in washing machines using centripetal force to push water outward through drum holes for efficient drying
High jump technique (Fosbury Flop) allows the athlete's center of mass to pass below the bar even as their body clears it
Calculating the mass of Earth from the known value of g at the surface and Earth's radius: M = gR²/G

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Universal Gravitation.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Universal Gravitation applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Tidal force analysis showing how the Moon's gravity varies across Earth's diameter, creating two tidal bulges on opposite sides
Foucault pendulum demonstration proving Earth's rotation by observing the slow precession of a long pendulum's swing plane over hours
Analyzing inclined conveyor belt forces in mining operations to determine motor power needed to transport ore uphill
Automotive engineering designing braking systems by analyzing deceleration forces and stopping distances using F = ma

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Universal Gravitation.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Universal Gravitation?

options.text: Elevator engineering calculating cable tension and motor force requirements for different loading conditions using F = ma for the elevator-passenger system
Spacecraft trajectory planning using Newton's Second Law with thrust force, gravitational force, and changing mass to compute acceleration profiles
Calculating energy requirements for elevators and conveyor systems using work against gravity W = mgh
GPS satellite corrections accounting for the variation of g with altitude to maintain precise timing and positioning

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Universal Gravitation.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Universal Gravitation in practice?

options.text: Hydroelectric dam design converting gravitational PE of water (mgh) to kinetic energy of turbines to electrical energy, analyzing efficiency at each conversion stage
Automotive engineering designing braking systems by analyzing deceleration forces and stopping distances using F = ma
Calculating the mass of Earth from the known value of g at the surface and Earth's radius: M = gR²/G
Seismometer design using physical pendulums with long periods to detect and record slow ground motion during earthquakes

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Universal Gravitation.
